title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies used in Plankton Ecology
Plankton systematics

The study of the classification and taxonomy of planktonic organisms.
It involves identifying and categorizing different species of plankton based on their morphological, physiological, ecological, and genetic characteristics of planktonic organisms.
This field helps us understand the diversity and evolutionary relationships among planktonic species, as well as their distribution patterns and ecological roles in aquatic ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton ecology

Plankton ecology is the study of planktonic organisms and their interactions with each other and their environment within aquatic ecosystems.
It includes the distribution, abundance, diversity, behavior, physiology, and ecological roles of plankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton productivity

Plankton productivity refers to the rate at which planktonic organisms produce organic matter through photosynthesis and other metabolic processes.
It is a critical component of aquatic ecosystems, as plankton are the primary producers that form the base of the food web and support the entire ecosystem.
Understanding plankton productivity is essential for understanding the dynamics of aquatic ecosystems and the impacts of human activities on these ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton?

Phytoplankton is derived from the Greek words:

Phyto – (plant)
Plankton — (wanderer or drifter)

Phytoplankton are microscopic aquatic plants which do not have ability to swim
They are free-floating aquatic organisms that depend entirely on water movement such as surface currents for their movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton…

They thrive in euphotic zone; the upper water column of the world’s oceans, lakes, rivers, ponds.
The euphotic zone receives energy from the sun that allows phytoplankton to photosynthesize – a chemical reaction that converts light energy into chemical energy (Peter et al., 2018).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Phytoplankton…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton Ecology?

Definition: Study of phytoplankton organisms and their interactions with each other and their aquatic environment
Key focus areas:

Distribution and abundance patterns
Physiological responses to environmental factors
Population dynamics and growth rates
Ecological roles in food webs and nutrient cycling
Community structure and biodiversity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology



Phytoplankton contain Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) – a green pigment responsible for photosynthesis
Chl-a captures light energy and converts it into chemical energy (glucose)
This is the foundation of all aquatic food webs

The Photosynthetic Equation:
\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\]

Key products:

Organic matter (glucose) - food for consumers
Oxygen (O₂) - byproduct for respiration
Accounts for ~50% of Earth’s oxygen production

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Productivity

Definition: Rate at which phytoplankton produce organic matter through photosynthesis
Two types:

Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total organic matter produced
Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Organic matter remaining after phytoplankton respiration

Global significance: Phytoplankton account for ~50% of total organic matter production on Earth

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Controlling Phytoplankton Growth
1. Light (Photosynthetically Active Radiation - PAR)

PAR is the portion of sunlight (400-700 nm) that phytoplankton can use for photosynthesis
Light is required for photosynthesis
It penetrates only upper 200 meters (euphotic zone)
Its intensity decreases with depth
Affects vertical distribution of phytoplankton
Limiting factor: In deep water and winter months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
2. Nutrient Availability


Macronutrients:

Nitrogen (N) - essential for proteins and nucleic acids
Phosphorus (P) - crucial for ATP and DNA production
Silica (Si) - needed by diatoms for cell walls
Carbon (C) - primary building block of photosynthesis


Micronutrients/Trace Elements:

Iron (Fe) - critical enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Manganese (Mn) - photosynthesis component
Zinc (Zn) - enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Copper (Cu) - respiration and photosynthesis component
Nutrient limitation: Occurs when essential nutrients become depleted
Some phytoplankton can fix nitrogen and can grow in areas where nitrate concentrations are low.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
3. Temperature

Affects metabolic rates and enzyme activity in phytoplankton
Optimal range: 15-30°C for most species, in tropics, optimal temperature is between 25-30°C
Seasonal effects: Growth varies with seasonal temperature changes
Geographic pattern: Higher productivity in temperate zones seasonally, but lower in tropical areas

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
4. Salinity

Determines osmotic balance for phytoplankton cells
Influences nutrient availability and uptake
Affects species composition (freshwater vs. marine species)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
5. Water Turbulence and Mixing

Vertical mixing: Brings nutrient-rich deeper water to surface
Reduces light availability: Deeper mixing = less average light exposure
Prevents sinking: Keeps phytoplankton in euphotic zone
Optimal conditions: Moderate mixing

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
6. Grazing Pressure

Predation by zooplankton: Controls phytoplankton biomass
Top-down control: High grazing = lower phytoplankton abundance
Species selectivity: Zooplankton prefer certain phytoplankton types

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutrient Limitation and the “Redfield Ratio”

Redfield Ratio: C:N:P = 106:16:1
Represents the average elemental composition of phytoplankton biomass
Used to predict which nutrient will limit growth

Application:

If actual N:P ratio < 16:1 → Nitrogen limited
If actual N:P ratio > 16:1 → Phosphorus limited
Helps predict ecosystem productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton Growth Phases: Overview

Phytoplankton populations follow a characteristic growth pattern with four distinct phases
Each phase represents different population dynamics
Understanding these phases is crucial for predicting bloom events and managing aquatic ecosystems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 1: Lag Phase (Adaptation)


Characteristics:

Phytoplankton acclimate to new environment
Low growth rate
Enzyme and nutrient uptake mechanisms are activated
Cells preparing for rapid growth

Duration:

Variable, typically days to weeks
Depends on environmental conditions


What is happening:

Cells synthesize enzymes
Photosynthetic machinery activated
Nutrient transport systems established
Building internal reserves

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 2: Exponential (Log) Growth Phase


Characteristics:

Rapid population growth under optimal conditions
Resources are abundant
Biomass doubles at regular intervals
Maximum growth rate achieved

Duration:

Weeks to months
Continues while resources available


What is happening:

Cells dividing at maximum rate
Nutrients abundant
Light optimal
Waste products not yet limiting
This is when algal blooms occur

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 3: Stationary Phase


Characteristics:

Growth rate equals death rate
Nutrient depletion begins
Waste accumulation occurs
Biomass plateaus at carrying capacity

Duration:

Variable, typically weeks
Reflects resource availability


What is happening:

Nutrient availability declining
Competition for resources intensifying
Toxin accumulation possible
Population stabilizes at maximum sustainable level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 4: Death (Decline) Phase


Characteristics:

Nutrients are exhausted
Toxic metabolites accumulate
Death rate exceeds birth rate
Population crashes

Duration:

Days to weeks
Depends on toxin levels


What is happening:

Essential nutrients depleted
Waste products toxic to cells
Cell lysis and death accelerates
Biomass rapidly decreases
Dead cells sink (detritus)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Phytoplankton Growth Curve

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Key Takeaways: Growth Phases









Phase
Growth Rate
Limiting Factors
Population Size




Lag
Low
Acclimation, enzyme synthesis
Slowly increasing


Exponential
Maximum
None (optimal conditions)
Rapidly doubling


Stationary
Zero
Nutrient depletion, waste
Constant (carrying capacity)


Death
Negative
Toxins, starvation
Rapidly declining

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Triggering Phase Transitions
Lag → Exponential:

Temperature stabilizes
Enzyme systems fully activated
Initial nutrient uptake

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Triggering Phase Transitions
Exponential → Stationary:

Key nutrients become limiting (N, P, Si, or Fe)
Waste metabolites accumulate
Light intensity decreases
Grazing pressure increases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Triggering Phase Transitions
Stationary → Death:

Complete nutrient depletion
Toxic compound buildup
pH changes
Oxygen depletion in enclosed systems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Biogeographic Distribution of Phytoplankton
Spatial Patterns:

Coastal waters (Neritic): HIGH productivity

Nutrient input from rivers and upwelling
Better light penetration

Open ocean (Oceanic): VARIABLE productivity

Often nutrient-poor (oligotrophic)
“Oceanic deserts” in tropical regions
High productivity in upwelling zones

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phyto. as Primary Producers
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ PHYTOPLANKTON (Primary Producers)               │
│ • Contain chlorophyll                           │
│ • Fix CO₂ into organic matter                   │
│ • Foundation of marine food webs                │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                 │ Consumed by
        ┌────────┴────────┐
    ┌───▼────────┐   ┌────▼─────────┐ 
    │Zooplankton │   │Filter feeders│
    └───┬────────┘   └───┬──────────┘
        └────────┬───────┘Consumed by
       ┌─────────▼─────────────┐
       │Fish & other consumers │
       └───────────────────────┘

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Primary Production:


Phytoplankton are primary producers, meaning they convert sunlight, carbon dioxide, and nutrients into organic matter through photosynthesis
They form the base of the marine food web, providing energy and nutrients to higher trophic levels.
Phytoplankton are the foundation of all aquatic food web

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Environmental Responses of Phytoplankton







Environmental Change
Phytoplankton Response




Increased nutrients
Biomass increase, potential bloom


Temperature rise
Species composition shift, range changes


Decreased light
Reduced productivity, migration to shallower water


Ocean acidification
Reduced calcification, cell size changes


Increased UV-B
Cellular damage, reduced photosynthesis


Salinity change
Species turnover, osmotic stress

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Indicator Species and Ecosystem Health

Phytoplankton as bioindicators:

Changes in species composition = ecosystem changes
Diversity patterns reflect environmental stress
Sensitive to pollution and nutrient enrichment

Eutrophication indicator:

High chlorophyll-a = nutrient pollution
Dominated by fast-growing species (e.g., cyanobacteria)

Climate change indicator:

Shifting species ranges and phenology
Changes in bloom timing and intensity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Phytoplankton

Biotechnological Applications:


Some species of phytoplankton are used in biotechnological applications, such as producing biofuels, pharmaceuticals, and nutritional supplements.
For example, certain types of microalgae are cultivated for their high lipid content, which can be converted into biodiesel.
Microalgae with the highest lipid content, ranging from 10 to 67%, belong to Chlorella, Dunaliella, and Scenedesmus species
A Spirulina species – a blue-green algae, holds immense potential in the field of biotechnology.
Its ability to produce valuable secondary metabolites like nutraceuticals and pharmaceuticals makes it a promising source for drug development
They are effective in managing allergies, cancer, and other diseases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
10. Aquaculture

Phytoplankton are a key food item in both aquaculture and mariculture.
Both utilize phytoplankton as food for the animals being farmed.
In mariculture, the phytoplankton is naturally occurring and is introduced into enclosures with the normal circulation of seawater.
In aquaculture, phytoplankton must be introduced directly.
The plankton can either be collected from a body of water or cultured.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
11. Economic Importance of Phytoplankton

Phytoplanktons have several uses and their mass cultivation can also be a major source of income.
They are mostly used as food supplements and play a vital role in both animal and human nutrition.
Phytoplanktons are also used in the manufacture of drugs.
Also, they have proven to be suitable for synthesizing vaccines.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Carbon flux in the ocean

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
12. Negative importance of phytoplankton

Some phytoplankton species can be the source of death or disease to other aquatic organisms.
Certain species of phytoplankton produce powerful biotoxins – leading to the development of red tides, or harmful algal blooms (HABs).
These toxic blooms can kill marine life and people who eat contaminated seafood.
Phytoplankton may cause mass mortality in other ways.
In the aftermath of a massive bloom, dead phytoplankton sink to the ocean or lake floor.
Then, decomposition occurs aided by bacteria.
This process lead to depletion of oxygen in the water, causing to suffocation of animals.
This may result to a massive death of aquatic organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Nitrogen fixation


Nitrogen fixation is the biological process by which certain microorganisms, primarily bacteria and cyanobacteria, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other nitrogen compounds that can be used by plants and other organisms.
They contribute significant amounts of fixed nitrogen to marine ecosystems.
This input of fixed nitrogen can alleviate nitrogen limitation in phytoplankton communities, promoting their growth and productivity.
Example of nitrogen fixers are such as Trichodesmium and Richelia species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Nutrient Cycling:


Phytoplankton contribute to nutrient cycling in marine ecosystems by recycling nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and iron.
They play a vital role in maintaining nutrient balance and productivity in marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Indicator of Ecosystem Health:


Changes in phytoplankton populations can serve as indicators of environmental changes and ecosystem health.
Monitoring phytoplankton abundance, species composition, and distribution can provide insights into water quality, nutrient levels, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Biotechnological Applications:


Some species of phytoplankton are used in biotechnological applications, such as producing biofuels, pharmaceuticals, and nutritional supplements.
For example, certain types of microalgae are cultivated for their high lipid content, which can be converted into biodiesel.
Microalgae with the highest lipid content, ranging from 10 to 67%, belong to Chlorella, Dunaliella, and Scenedesmus species
A Spirulina species – a blue-green algae, holds immense potential in the field of biotechnology.
Its ability to produce valuable secondary metabolites like nutraceuticals and pharmaceuticals makes it a promising source for drug development
They are effective in managing allergies, cancer, and other diseases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Climate Regulation:


Phytoplankton influence climate by reducing atmospheric CO2 levels during photosynthesis process

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton

Oxygen Production:


Phytoplankton produce a significant portion of the Earth’s oxygen through photosynthesis, contributing more than 50% of the oxygen in the Earth’s atmosphere.
They play a crucial role in maintaining global oxygen levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
10. Aquaculture

Phytoplankton are a key food item in both aquaculture and mariculture.
Both utilize phytoplankton as food for the animals being farmed.
In mariculture, the phytoplankton is naturally occurring and is introduced into enclosures with the normal circulation of seawater.
In aquaculture, phytoplankton must be obtained and introduced directly.
The plankton can either be collected from a body of water or cultured.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
11. Economic Importance of Phytoplankton

Phytoplanktons have several uses and their mass cultivation can also be a major source of income.
They are mostly used as food supplements and play a vital role in both animal and human nutrition.
Phytoplanktons are also used in the manufacture of drugs.
Also, they have proven to be suitable for synthesizing vaccines.

text:
Phytoplanktons and their uses

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
12. Negative importance of phytoplankton

Some phytoplankton species can be the source of death or disease to other aquatic organisms.
Certain species of phytoplankton produce powerful biotoxins – leading to the development of red tides, or harmful algal blooms (HABs).
These toxic blooms can kill marine life and people who eat contaminated seafood.
Phytoplankton may cause mass mortality in other ways.
In the aftermath of a massive bloom, dead phytoplankton sink to the ocean or lake floor.
Then, decomposition occurs aided by bacteria.
This process lead to depletion of oxygen in the water, causing to suffocation of animals.
This may result to a massive death of aquatic organisms.

text: Dead fish washed to a beach at Padre Island, Texas, in October 2009, following a red tide (HABs)

text:
References




Peter, N., Semba, M., Lugomela, C., Kyewalyanga, M.S., 2018. The influence of physical-chemical variables on the spatial and seasonal variation of chlorophyll-a in coastal waters of unguja, zanzibar, tanzania. Western Indian Ocean Journal of Marine Science 17, 25–34.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton



The word plankton comes from a Greek word planktos, meaning drifter or wanderer.
Aquatic organisms that are incapable of swimming or moving on their own.
Their movement in water is aided by water movement such as tides, currents, waves
Most plankton are microscopic, often less than one inch in length.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton…



They also include larger species like some crustaceans and jellyfish.
Some organisms are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Others are planktonic only when they are young, but they eventually grow large enough to swim against the currents.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of Plankton

Plankton can be classified in several ways based on their;

Size
Life cycle
Trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on size

Based on their size, plankton can be classified as

Megaplankton – > 20 cm eg. Jellyfish
Macroplankton – 2-20 cm eg. Pteropods, krill
Mesoplankton – 0.2 mm-2 cm eg. copepods, Foraminifera
Microplankton – 20-200 \({\mu}m\) eg. Ciliates, coccolithophores
Nanoplankton – 2-20 \({\mu}m\) eg. Diatoms, dinoflagellates
Picoplankton – 0.2-2 \({\mu}m\) eg. smaller eukaryotic protists, bacteria
Femtoplankton – < 0.2 \({\mu}m\) eg. marine viruses

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Depending on mode of life cycle, plankton can be classified as:

Holoplankton or
Meroplankton

Holoplankton are organisms that are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Holoplankton lives in pelagic zone in aquatic environment

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Pelagic organisms are divided into two groups; plankton and nekton.
Nektons are capable of swimming against water current and include most species of sharks, rays, fishes, seals, sea lions, and whales.
Examples of holoplankton are some diatoms, radiolarians, some dinoflagellates, foraminifera, amphipods, krill, copepods, and salps, as well as some gastropod mollusk species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Meroplankton are aquatic organisms which have both planktonic and benthic stages in their life cycles.
Many of the meroplankton consists of larval stages of larger organism (Stübner et al., 2016)
After a period of time in the plankton, many meroplankton changes to the nekton or adopt a benthic (often sessile) lifestyle on the seafloor.
Many benthic community are planktonic during their larval stages (Ershova et al., 2019)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

The planktonic larval stage is particularly crucial to many benthic invertebrate in order to disperse their young.
Depending on particular species and environmental conditions, larval or juvenile-stage meroplankton may remain in the pelagic zone for duration ranging from an hour to months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on Trophic level

Plankton can also be divided based on the trophic level
This is the most basic category that divide plankton into two groups;

Phytoplankton – plants and
Zooplankton – animals.

Zooplankton and other small marine organisms feed on phytoplankton and they become food for fish, crustaceans, and other larger species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distribution of plankton

Plankton inhabits oceans, seas, lakes and ponds.
Their abundance varies both horizontally, vertically and seasonally.
This is due to availability of light.
Another factor causing plankton variation is nutrient availability.
Although large areas of tropical and sub-tropical oceans have abundant light, but they experience relatively low primary production because they offer limited nutrients such as nitrate, phosphate, iron and silicate.
In such regions, primary production usually occurs at greater depth, but at a reduced level due to limited nutrient level.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Aquatic environment divisions

Aquatic ecosystems can be sub-divided into:

Vertical division
Horizontal division

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical division

This has two sub-groups:

Benthic

Entire bottom, or the substratum and its attached and crawling organisms on the substratum.

Pelagic

Entire water body excluding the benthic system; It deals with floating and swimming organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal division

Two major parts:

Neritic province: – Shallow water above the continental shelf.
Oceanic: – Deep water beyond the continental shelf.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Aquatic environment divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
References




Ershova, E., Descoteaux, R., Wangensteen, O., Iken, K., Hopcroft, R., Smoot, C., Grebmeier, J.M., Bluhm, B.A., 2019. Diversity and distribution of meroplanktonic larvae in the pacific arctic and connectivity with adult benthic invertebrate communities. Frontiers in Marine Science 6, 490.


Stübner, E., Søreide, J., Reigstad, M., Marquardt, M., Blachowiak-Samolyk, K., 2016. Year-round meroplankton dynamics in high-arctic svalbard. Journal of Plankton Research 38, 522–536.

title: About
text: About this site

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Objectives
To introduce students to the systematics of both phytoplankton and zooplankton; plankton sampling methods and determination of productivity; plankton ecology and productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Synopsis/Modules

Module 1: Course Introduction

Introduction to plankton and their importance in aquatic ecosystems.
Classification of Plankton
Division of the aquatic environment



Module 2: Plankton Ecology

Systematic and biology of phytoplankton and Zooplankton (holoplankton and meroplankton).
Adaptation to the environment:

Structural adaptations to floatation, aids of floatation
Cyclomorphosis, colour and transparency, bioluminescence




Module 3: Plankton Productivity

Primary production in the aquatic environment.
Secondary production and the food webs.
Bacterial production and the concept of the microbial loop.
Factors controlling the distribution and productivity.
Nutrient regeneration and the role of zooplankton in nutrient cycling.
Trophodynamic relationships.
Feeding processes: rate of filtering, prey selection.
Zooplankton variations and the concept of indicator species.
Zoogeography of the holoplankton.
Vertical migration: seasonal, ontogenic and diel-vertical migration.
Spatial and seasonal changes of phytoplankton and zooplankton.



Module 4: Harmful Microalgae

Harmful algal blooms (HABs): causes, effects, and management strategies.
Types of HABs



Module 5: Sampling Methods and Laboratory Analysis

Methods of measurement of biomass and primary production, bacterial production, and nitrogen fixation in the water column.
Quantitative phytoplankton and zooplankton sampling; methods of collecting, fixing and preserving.
Laboratory analyses of planktons.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Key Textbooks

Ecology of Phytoplankton (Reynolds, 2006)
Zooplankton methodology manual (Harris et al., 2000)
Zooplankton of the Atlantic and Gulf Coasts (Boxshall, 2014)
Plankton and Productivity in the Oceans (Raymont, 2014)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course assessment

Test1 = 10 marks
Test2 = 15 marks
Assignments = 5 marks
Practicals = 10 marks
University examination (UE) = 60 marks

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division

Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division



The ocean environment is divided based on:

Depth (vertical zones)
Distance from shore (horizontal zones)

These divisions help us understand the distribution of plankton and marine organisms
Each zone has unique characteristics affecting life forms





Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical Zones: Pelagic Division

Pelagic zone: The entire water column (open water)
Divided into distinct depth zones based on light penetration and pressure

Major Pelagic Zones:

Epipelagic (Euphotic) – 0-200 m
Mesopelagic (Twilight) – 200-1000 m
Bathypelagic (Midnight) – 1000-6000 m
Hadopelagic (Abyssal) – >6000 m

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Epipelagic Zone (Sunlight Zone)

Depth: 0-200 meters
Characteristics:

Receives sunlight
Warmest zone
Supports photosynthesis
Highest biodiversity
Dissolved oxygen abundant

Plankton: Rich in phytoplankton and zooplankton
Importance: Primary production occurs here

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Mesopelagic Zone (Twilight Zone)

Depth: 200-1000 meters
Characteristics:

Dim light (partial darkness)
Twilight conditions (not enough for photosynthesis)
Rapid temperature decrease
Lower photosynthesis

Plankton: Specialized zooplankton adapted to low light
Organisms: Bioluminescent species common

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Bathypelagic Zone (Midnight Zone)

Depth: 1000-6000 meters
Characteristics:

Complete darkness
Cold temperatures (2-4°C)
High pressure
Low oxygen in some regions

Plankton: Sparse plankton communities
Energy source: Marine snow (falling organic matter)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Hadopelagic Zone (Abyssal Zone)

Depth: >6000 meters
Characteristics:

Extreme darkness
Crushing pressure
Near-freezing temperatures
Minimal life

Plankton: Extremely specialized and rare
Organisms: Found near hydrothermal vents

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal Zones: Neritic vs. Oceanic
Neritic Province:

Shallow water above the continental shelf
Extends 200+ km from shore
High nutrient availability
High productivity
Supports rich plankton communities

Oceanic Province:

Deep water beyond the continental shelf
Low nutrient availability (except upwelling zones)
Lower productivity in many regions
Unique specialized plankton

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Benthic Division

Benthic zone: The seafloor and organisms living on/in it
Divided by depth:

Supralittoral – Above high tide
Littoral – Intertidal zone
Sublittoral – Continental shelf (< 200 m)
Abyssal – Deep ocean floor

Meroplankton larvae settle in benthic zones

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Key Factors Affecting Ocean Zones



Factor
Impact




Light
Enables photosynthesis; affects animal behavior


Temperature
Controls metabolic rates and organism distribution


Pressure
Affects gas solubility and organism physiology


Dissolved Oxygen
Essential for respiration; varies by depth


Nutrients
Crucial for primary production; limits growth


Salinity
Affects osmotic balance in organisms

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton Distribution by Zone


Epipelagic:

Rich plankton communities
Maximum phytoplankton
Diverse zooplankton
Larval forms

Mesopelagic:

Deep-sea copepods
Larval fishes
Myctophids (lanternfish)


Bathypelagic:

Specialized zooplankton
Detritus feeders
Bioluminescent species

Hadopelagic:

Hadal amphipods
Sparse copepods
Specialized bacteria

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Ocean Divisions

Scientific Understanding:

Predicting organism distribution
Understanding adaptation strategies
Tracking nutrient cycling

Conservation:

Managing marine resources
Understanding climate impacts
Protecting biodiversity

Fisheries:

Locating commercial species
Sustainable harvest practices

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Summary

Ocean divisions provide a framework for understanding marine ecosystems
Vertical divisions (epipelagic, mesopelagic, bathypelagic, hadopelagic) depend on depth and light
Horizontal divisions (neritic, oceanic) depend on distance from shore
Each zone has unique physical conditions
Plankton distributions reflect these environmental gradients

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: References

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis…
The Photosynthetic Equation:
\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\] \[Carbon dioxide + Water \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} Glucose + Oxygen\]

This process is catalyzed by sunlight and chlorophyll in phytoplankton cells
It is the primary mechanism by which energy enters aquatic ecosystems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration:

Phytoplankton absorb CO₂ from the atmosphere during photosynthesis and incorporate it into their biomass.
Through photosynthesis, phytoplankton consume carbon dioxide on a scale equivalent to forests and other land plants.
Some of this carbon is carried to the deep ocean when phytoplankton die – effectively removing carbon from the atmosphere and contributing to the regulation of climate.
Some is transferred to different layers of the ocean as phytoplankton are eaten by other creatures, which themselves reproduce, generate waste, and die.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration…:

Worldwide, this biological carbon pump transfers about 10 gigatonnes of carbon from the atmosphere to the deep ocean each year.
Even a small changes in the growth of phytoplankton may affect atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations, which would feed back to global surface temperatures.
How ????

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Food Source:


Phytoplankton are consumed by a wide range of marine organisms, including zooplankton, small fish, shellfish, and whales.
They feed all aquatic organisms from microscopic, animal-like zooplankton to multi-ton whales.
Some small fish and invertebrates they also graze on the phytoplankton, and then those smaller animals are eaten by bigger ones.
They are essential food sources for many marine species, forming the foundation of marine food webs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nitrogen fixation


Nitrogen fixation is the biological process by which certain microorganisms, primarily bacteria and cyanobacteria, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other nitrogen compounds that can be used by plants and other organisms.
They contribute significant amounts of fixed nitrogen to marine ecosystems.
This input of fixed nitrogen can alleviate nitrogen limitation in phytoplankton communities, promoting their growth and productivity.
Example of nitrogen fixers are such as Trichodesmium and Richelia species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nutrient Cycling:


Phytoplankton contribute to nutrient cycling in marine ecosystems by recycling nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and iron.
They play a vital role in maintaining nutrient balance and productivity in marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Indicator of Ecosystem Health:


Changes in phytoplankton populations can serve as indicators of environmental changes and ecosystem health.
Monitoring phytoplankton abundance, species composition, and distribution can provide insights into water quality, nutrient levels, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Climate Regulation:


Phytoplankton influence climate by reducing atmospheric CO2 levels during photosynthesis process

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Oxygen Production:


Phytoplankton produce a significant portion of oxygen on the Earth through photosynthesis, contributing more than 50% of the oxygen in the Earth’s atmosphere.
They play a major role in maintaining global oxygen levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Biogeographic Distribution of Phytoplankton
Temporal Patterns:

Seasonal cycles: Spring and fall blooms in temperate zones
Monsoon-driven cycles: In tropical regions with seasonal rains
Daily vertical migration: Some species move up/down
Interannual variability: Related to climate patterns (El Niño, etc.)
